stops.name: Dam Square
stops.shortDescription: The historical heart of Amsterdam, deriving its name from its original function: a dam on the Amstel River. It is a lively hub often hosting fairs, performers, and public gatherings.

stops.name: Royal Palace of Amsterdam
stops.shortDescription: Originally built as a city hall during the Dutch Golden Age in the 17th century, resting on 13,659 wooden piles. It later became the royal palace of King Louis Napoleon and later the Dutch Royal House.

stops.name: Magna Plaza
stops.shortDescription: A stunning, ornate Neo-Gothic building located just behind the Royal Palace. It functioned as the Amsterdam Main Post Office before being beautifully converted into a luxurious shopping mall.

stops.name: Westerkerk
stops.shortDescription: A magnificent 17th-century Protestant church featuring the highest church tower in Amsterdam. Its bells were frequently mentioned by Anne Frank in her diary as a source of comfort.

stops.name: Anne Frank House
stops.shortDescription: The writer's house and biographical museum where Anne Frank and her family hid from Nazi persecution during World War II. The concealed 'Secret Annex' has been preserved exactly as it was.

stops.name: De 9 Straatjes (The 9 Streets)
stops.shortDescription: A highly photogenic neighborhood of picturesque canal streets intersected by historic bridges. It's famous for its independent boutiques, cozy cafes, and classic Dutch step-gabled houses.

stops.name: Begijnhof
stops.shortDescription: A serene, hidden courtyard originally built in the 14th century for the Beguines. It contains the oldest surviving wooden house in Amsterdam.

stops.name: Spui
stops.shortDescription: A bustling square famous for its weekly book and art markets, surrounded by popular cafes and independent bookstores.

stops.name: Bloemenmarkt
stops.shortDescription: The world's only floating flower market, situated on the Singel canal, offering a vibrant display of tulips and bulbs since 1862.

stops.name: Munttoren
stops.shortDescription: Originally part of the city gate, this historic 'Mint Tower' was used to mint coins in the 17th century and features a beautiful carillon.

stops.name: Rembrandtplein
stops.shortDescription: One of the busiest squares in the city, named after the famous painter Rembrandt van Rijn, and featuring a cast-iron monument in his honor.

stops.name: Magere Brug
stops.shortDescription: The most famous drawbridge in Amsterdam, crossing the Amstel River. It is beautifully illuminated at night by 1,200 light bulbs.

stops.name: Rijksmuseum
stops.shortDescription: The national museum of the Netherlands, housing a vast collection of Dutch Golden Age masterpieces, including works by Rembrandt and Vermeer.

stops.name: Van Gogh Museum
stops.shortDescription: Located in Museumplein, this museum contains the largest collection of paintings and drawings by Vincent van Gogh in the world.

stops.name: Vondelpark
stops.shortDescription: The largest city park in Amsterdam, offering a peaceful green oasis with English-style gardens, ponds, and open-air theater performances.

stops.name: Leidseplein
stops.shortDescription: A vibrant center for nightlife and entertainment, surrounded by theaters, cinemas, and lively terraces to conclude the walking tour.
